authoritative_sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual, Sector 56 and Subsector 561
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Census Bureau and Office of Management and Budget
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The NAICS manual is the official U.S. industry classification baseline. It defines administrative and support services as routine day-to-day support activities and distinguishes contract or fee-basis support from strategic management and public administration.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Named-industry scope, service boundary, and artifact vocabulary
used_for: refusal when support work becomes client management or single-service specialist work

authoritative_sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual, 561110 Office Administrative Services
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Census Bureau and Office of Management and Budget
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NAICS 561110 is authoritative for day-to-day office administrative services such as billing and recordkeeping, personnel, and logistics performed for others on a contract or fee basis without operating the complete business.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Office administrative service request framing and contract-boundary review
used_for: overgeneralization boundary against complete operations ownership

authoritative_sources.title: Administrative Services and Facilities Managers
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: BLS is a federal labor-statistics authority. Its occupational guidance is authoritative for public terminology around administrative services work, office procedures, recordkeeping, supplies, and support coordination.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Administrative service intake, status tracking, and procedure-aligned maintenance
used_for: scope separation from specialized HR, finance, and facilities decisions

authoritative_sources.title: Federal Records Management
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NARA is the U.S. federal records authority. Its public records-management guidance is authoritative for records policy, records officer context, recordkeeping topics, and lifecycle governance used as a public records-discipline baseline.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Records lifecycle, records officer handoff, and official-record governance
used_for: retention and records officer escalation posture
recordkeeping topics such as email, electronic messages, essential records, and agency records contacts

authoritative_sources.title: Disposition of Federal Records: A Records Management Handbook
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NARA's disposition handbook is authoritative for records creation, maintenance and use, disposition, records schedules, transfer, destruction, and approved disposition authority. It directly constrains retention, disposal, and publication handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Retention, disposition, transfer, and destruction handling
used_for: refusal of ad hoc deletion or reclassification

authoritative_sources.title: Unauthorized Dispositions
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NARA guidance is authoritative for handling allegations of unlawful removal, alteration, deletion, destruction, corruption, or other unauthorized disposition of records. It supports failure handling and escalation for unsupported record changes.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Unauthorized change, corruption, deletion, or destruction escalation
used_for: escalation when record integrity is threatened

authoritative_sources.title: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations, SP 800-53 Rev. 5
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 is an authoritative public control catalog for information systems and organizations. It supports access control, audit and accountability, privacy, identification, and integrity requirements for systems of record used by this lane.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Access control, audit logging, privacy, and system evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: 36 CFR Part 1222, Creation and Maintenance of Federal Records
authoritative_sources.publisher: Electronic Code of Federal Regulations and National Archives and Records Administration
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The eCFR publishes current federal regulations, and NARA regulations are authoritative for federal record creation and maintenance. The part supports official-record controls, documentation, and maintenance expectations that constrain administrative record updates.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Recordkeeping governance, terminology normalization, and evidence-quality review
used_for: record update boundary and evidence posture

workflow_stages.name: Administrative request intake and scope classification
workflow_stages.description: Confirm requester identity, client or business unit, service category, queue owner, SLA, requested outcome, and whether the request is routine NAICS 561 administrative support, single-service specialist work, or client management authority.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Source record and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate required source records, attachments, approvals, record locations, tenant policies, service catalog references, retention class, access permissions, and retrieval hooks before record maintenance or completion language.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Administrative maintenance and status tracking
workflow_stages.description: Prepare permitted record updates, document completeness summaries, administrative service status notes, queue blocker summaries, and evidence packets using source records and system audit requirements.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Retention, access, and publication or handoff handling
workflow_stages.description: Apply documented retention, privacy, legal hold, access, disclosure, transfer, publication, and official-record owner constraints. Escalate when the lane lacks authority to dispose, publish, reclassify, or disclose a record.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Conflict, missing-authority, and adjacent-lane escalation
workflow_stages.description: Stop when records conflict, tenant policy and public guidance conflict, evidence is missing, or requested action crosses into legal, finance, HR, compliance, privacy, records governance, security, safety, or client management authority.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: administrative service request, ticket, queue item, SLA state, priority, requester identity, and client or business unit context
client service catalog, statement of work, runbook, administrative procedure, approval matrix, escalation matrix, and exception policy
intake forms, source records, attachments, correspondence, policy acknowledgments, signed approvals, change requests, and evidence binders
document repository metadata, record classification, retention schedule, legal hold flag, privacy label, access role, and disclosure restriction
case-management status, workflow state, owner confirmation, document completeness checklist, queue aging sheet, and blocker log
system audit logs, update history, field ownership map, source hierarchy, and retrieval hook references
record update note, document or evidence packet, administrative status note, retention or access escalation package, and audit-trail or exception summary
systems_of_record: workflow queue or shared-services work management system
identity, access, and entitlement management system
client contract, SOW, and service catalog repository
audit log or GRC evidence repository
communication archive for email, chat, and correspondence records

allowed: Classify administrative service requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Request or validate the minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, and source context required to continue safely.
Determine whether records are complete, current, properly classified, and ready for the requested administrative use.
Prepare permitted record update notes, administrative status notes, evidence packets, and handoff summaries without overstating authority.
Route the work to the correct adjacent specialist, approver, official-record owner, or human reviewer when ownership shifts.
escalate: Required tenant facts, source records, attachments, approval evidence, retention schedule, access model, or retrieval hooks are missing.
Records conflict across systems of record or source hierarchy is unclear.
Retention, privacy, access, legal hold, publication, transfer, or official-record questions exceed the lane's administrative authority.
Public guidance, tenant policy, contract terms, or client instructions conflict materially.
The request asks for legal, compliance, HR, finance, safety, privacy, information-security, or client management decision authority.
refuse: Approve, backdate, delete, dispose, disclose, publish, or reclassify an official record without documented authority and audit requirements.
Decide the underlying case, claim, benefit, contract, employment, legal, compliance, finance, privacy, or safety outcome.
Bypass required attachments, approvals, retention controls, access permissions, or audit trails to close a queue item.
Treat administrative support and business services as authority to operate the complete client business or make strategic policy decisions.

domain_failure_modes: the pack stays generic and does not encode NAICS 561 support-service boundaries, contract or fee-basis scope, client records, or queue ownership
the lane updates or certifies records without required source evidence, attachments, permissions, or audit trail
administrative maintenance is mistaken for authority to approve the underlying business, finance, HR, legal, compliance, or client management outcome
retention, privacy, access, legal hold, or official-record ownership conflicts are resolved informally instead of escalated
conflicting systems of record are synthesized into confident completion language without source-of-truth resolution
tenant-specific terms and artifacts are normalized too aggressively and lose material support-service assumptions or confidentiality controls
client support scope is widened into complete outsourced operations, strategic management, or a more specialized adjacent lane

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Required record or evidence package is incomplete
Retention, privacy, or access rule conflicts with the request
Stakeholder asks the lane to approve an official record decision it cannot own

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific retention, access, confidentiality, contract scope, source hierarchy, and official-record ownership vary materially by client, jurisdiction, service model, and system configuration.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The public baseline establishes administrative support-service boundaries and records discipline. Runtime must retrieve tenant policy, service catalog, contract, retention schedule, access model, and system mappings before acting on a specific client record.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Administrative support services can overlap with HR, finance, facilities, legal operations, IT access, compliance, and records governance depending on the tenant service catalog.
unresolved_ambiguities.notes: The pack resolves this by requiring service catalog and adjacent owner mapping. If ownership cannot be identified, the lane returns to orchestrator instead of absorbing adjacent work.

notes: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated spec package because tenant-specific variation is converted into explicit retrieval, refusal, and handoff requirements.

The research baseline intentionally combines NAICS 561 named-industry guidance, federal administrative-services terminology, NARA records workflow, NIST system-control expectations, and eCFR/NARA record-creation and maintenance controls.
The lane remains specification-based and does not claim retained implementation behavior.
